one_line_takeaway: TrustRAG is a training-free, two-stage RAG defense (embedding-cluster filtering + internal-knowledge conflict resolution) that drives corpus-poisoning ASR toward single digits even at 100% poison rate, where majority-vote defenses break down.
key_finding: TrustRAG (stage 1&2) substantially reduces ASR (paper claims up to ~80% reduction) while improving ACC (up to ~30%), outperforming RobustRAG, InstructRAG, and AstuteRAG across three datasets and three LLMs. The two stages are complementary: Stage 1 alone can even preserve malicious content deliberately (e.g., NQ/AD on Llama3.1-8B: stage 1 = 45.0 ACC / 52.0 ASR), but stage 1&2 jumps to 82.0 ACC / 4.0 ASR. Critically, against multiple-injection attacks at 100% poison rate (NQ, Llama3.1-8B), TrustRAG holds 83.0 ACC / 2.0 ASR while Vanilla RAG collapses to 2.0/98.0 and the strongest baseline AstuteRAG reaches only 61.0/29.0 — demonstrating robustness precisely where majority-vote defenses fail. Runtime cost is ~2x Vanilla RAG (3 API calls for the conflict stage), far cheaper than RobustRAG's ~11-12x (11 API calls).
core_contribution: TrustRAG is a training-free, plug-and-play defense framework that protects Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines against corpus-poisoning attacks by filtering malicious and irrelevant retrieved documents before generation. Its own novel contribution is a two-stage defense: Stage 1 (Clean Retrieval) uses K-means clustering (k=2) over document embeddings to isolate the tight cluster formed by multiple injected malicious documents, plus a ROUGE-L n-gram-preservation check to avoid discarding clean documents in single-injection cases; Stage 2 (Conflict Resolution) extracts the LLM's internal knowledge (single inference at temperature 0), consolidates it with external documents, and runs a self-assessment step that lets the LLM pick the most well-supported answer source. The authors position it as the first framework designed to defend against BOTH single- and multiple-document injection attacks, unlike prior majority-vote defenses (RobustRAG) that fail once malicious documents outnumber clean ones.
threat_model: An attacker chooses a set of target questions and, for each, a desired incorrect answer (e.g., q='Who is the CEO of OpenAI?', target r='Tim Cook'). The attacker injects N malicious documents per question into the external knowledge base. The attack succeeds when the poisoned corpus D∪Γ causes the LLM to output the attacker's incorrect answer. The attacker optimizes malicious text S⊕I to maximize embedding similarity with the target query (to guarantee retrieval into top-k) while keeping the answer payload I likely to be generated, subject to an embedding-distance constraint ϵ to preserve retrievability. Attacker capabilities are strong/white-box: access to the external database, the retriever, and open-source LLM weights, enabling tailored malicious documents (realized in practice by editing Wikipedia, fake news, etc.). The defense objective is to minimize Attack Success Rate (ASR) subject to keeping Accuracy (ACC) above a threshold, explicitly avoiding over-rejection.
limitations: The K-means (k=2) filtering premise depends on malicious documents forming a tight embedding cluster; the paper's own Figure 3 shows this only holds reliably when the number of malicious docs exceeds ~2, and single dispersed injections must rely on the weaker Stage-2 defense. The threat model assumes a strong white-box attacker but the defense is only tested against a fixed set of attack methods (PoisonedRAG, PIA, AD, Jamming); an adaptive attacker who deliberately diversifies malicious embeddings to defeat clustering is not evaluated. Stage 2 leans heavily on the LLM's internal knowledge being correct, which can fail on long-tail or post-cutoff queries and risks the LLM overriding correct external evidence. Results are averaged over 100 queries per dataset (small evaluation set), and hard k=2 clustering can mistakenly filter clean documents at low poison rates (20%), which the ROUGE/n-gram mechanism only partially mitigates. Some numbers are self-reported peak improvements (up to 80%/30%) rather than average gains.

quantitative_results: Headline claims: reduces attack success rate by up to 80% and improves response accuracy by up to 30% vs prior defenses
NQ, AD attack, Llama3.1-8B: TrustRAG stage 1 = 45.0 ACC / 52.0 ASR; stage 1&2 = 82.0 ACC / 4.0 ASR
Single-injection HotpotQA under PIA: baseline defenses' ASR ranges 4.0%-64.0% while TrustRAG stays 3.0%-9.0%
Multiple-injection NQ (Llama3.1-8B) at 100% poison: TrustRAG stage 1&2 = 83.0 ACC / 2.0 ASR vs AstuteRAG 61.0/29.0, RobustRAG 11.0/83.0, Vanilla RAG 2.0/98.0
Multiple-injection MS-MARCO (Llama3.1-8B) at 100% poison: TrustRAG stage 1&2 = 87.0 ACC / 5.0 ASR vs Vanilla RAG 3.0/97.0
Single-injection GPT4o on MS-MARCO/PIA: TrustRAG stage 1&2 = 91.0 ACC / 1.0 ASR
Runtime (Llama3.1-8B, 100 queries): TrustRAG conflict stage 3 API calls, ~2.1-2.3x Vanilla RAG; RobustRAG 11 API calls, ~11-12x; TrustRAG K-means stage 1 API call, ~1.3-1.4x
TrustRAG maintains average accuracy above 80% across poison rates 20%-100% in the multi-injection setting
